title: Cloudflare Details Unified Data Platform Where Billing Workloads Account for 53% of Queries
gist: Cloudflare details Town Lake, an internal Trino/Iceberg/R2 lakehouse unifying fragmented operational, billing, security and business data, plus Skipper, a natural-language AI agent on top. Billing workloads dominate usage at 53% of queries, and the design centers on default-closed governance and enforcement placed close to execution.

insights.claim: Cloudflare built an internal unified data platform called Town Lake on a lakehouse stack of Apache Trino, Apache Iceberg, Cloudflare R2 object storage, and DataHub for metadata, exposing a single SQL interface across previously fragmented systems.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Signals continued enterprise consolidation onto open lakehouse formats (Iceberg/Trino) over proprietary warehouses; watch Iceberg-ecosystem vendors and object-storage economics.
insights.locator: The platform is built on a lakehouse architecture

insights.claim: A single Town Lake query can join data across Postgres, ClickHouse, and Iceberg tables without moving data between systems, replacing prior copies scattered across Postgres, ClickHouse, Kafka, BigQuery, and object storage.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Query-federation without ETL erodes the case for data-movement/pipeline tooling; watch ELT and reverse-ETL vendors facing substitution risk.
insights.locator: Cloudflare stated that a single query can join data

insights.claim: Cloudflare's global network processes more than one billion events per second across over 330 cities in 120 countries, the data-volume driver behind needing a unified platform.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Quantifies the telemetry scale that justifies bespoke internal data infrastructure at edge-network operators.
insights.locator: According to Cloudflare, its global network

insights.claim: Cloudflare expects to migrate additional Town Lake workloads onto R2 SQL as the system matures, and is expanding a Transformer pipeline so teams define curated datasets via SQL and metadata files that are auto-deployed, monitored, and cataloged through DataHub.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Indicates Cloudflare is productizing R2 SQL internally first, a possible precursor to a commercial zero-egress analytics offering.
insights.locator: Looking ahead, Cloudflare plans deeper integration

insights.claim: Billing workloads account for 53% of all queries on Cloudflare's Town Lake platform, making billing the majority use case ahead of security, support, and business intelligence.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Reveals that the highest-value internal AI-analytics use case is revenue/billing operations, not the flashier security or BI framing vendors emphasize.
insights.locator: Billing workloads represent a majority of usage

insights.claim: Town Lake processed 91,760 billing-related queries from 324 employees in a measured period, spanning billing analysis, support investigations, and operational reporting.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Concrete adoption metric (~283 queries per user) showing internal AI-data tools reach broad non-specialist usage once query friction is removed.

insights.claim: Cloudflare built an AI agent called Skipper on top of Town Lake that translates plain-English requests into validated SQL queries using metadata, schema definitions, transformation lineage, documentation, and runtime inspection.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Text-to-SQL agents grounded in lineage and metadata are becoming standard internal tooling; watch BI/analytics vendors that lack a governed semantic layer.
insights.locator: On top of Town Lake, Cloudflare built Skipper

insights.claim: Cloudflare found that simplifying AI agent prompts improved accuracy, consolidating overlapping tools reduced incorrect tool selections, and feeding SQL transformation logic and lineage into the agent context improved understanding of business semantics beyond raw schema metadata.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Actionable engineering lessons: fewer tools and lineage-as-context beat prompt complexity, a template for teams building enterprise data agents.
insights.locator: The company also reported that simplifying AI agent prompts

insights.claim: Cloudflare plans to embed Skipper deeper into internal chat, ticketing, and development workflows, moving the agent from a query tool toward an embedded operational assistant.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Distribution of AI agents into existing workflow surfaces (chat/ticketing) is the adoption vector to watch for enterprise agent platforms.
insights.locator: Looking ahead, Cloudflare plans deeper integration

insights.claim: Town Lake uses a default-closed governance model where newly onboarded datasets stay inaccessible until automated scanning and human review are complete, enforced via an internal service, Skimmer, that combines automated classification with AI-based analysis to detect sensitive data and PII.
insights.speaker: Leela Kumili
insights.so_what: Default-closed plus AI-assisted PII classification is emerging as the governance baseline for AI-accessible data lakes; a spec buyers will demand.
insights.locator: A key design element is its default closed governance model

insights.claim: Putting an AI agent that can reason across operational data on top of a unified analytics platform forces enforcement to sit close to execution via distributed deterministic checks, or the platform becomes an uncontrolled action surface.
insights.speaker: Patrick Joubert
insights.so_what: Frames the core risk in agentic data platforms; guardrail/policy-enforcement tooling near execution is an investable category as agents get write access.
insights.quote: If the agent can reason across operational data, enforcement has to sit close to execution. Distributed deterministic checks are what let the agent act without turning the data platform into an uncontrolled action surface.
insights.locator: Patrick Joubert, CEO of Rippletide, commented
